notes: Integrated from the real Grade 2 Quarter 2, Book 12 source file.
Developing card: moderate length with more vocabulary and plot detail.

book.title: Thumbelina
book.description: Thumbelina includes a six-chapter reading sequence, vocabulary, comprehension questions, journal/writing prompts, and assessment checks.

choiceGuidance.reason: moderate length with more vocabulary and plot detail

weeks.title: Thumbelina Book Card
vocabulary: 1. cherish - to love and take care of something very precious
2. miniature - very small; a tiny version of something
1. stranded - left alone in a place with no way to escape
2. gnawed - chewed or bit through something
1. disapprovingly - in a way that shows you don't like or approve of something
2. criticized - said negative things about someone or something
1. desperate - in a very bad situation with little hope
2. arrangement - an agreement or plan between people
1. sympathy - feeling sorry for someone who is suffering
2. vividly - in a very clear and detailed way
2. transparent - clear; you can see through it
journal: Write about a place Thumbelina visits and how it makes her feel.
Explain how Thumbelina shows courage even though she is small.
writing: Write about a place Thumbelina visits and how it makes her feel.
Explain how Thumbelina shows courage even though she is small.
questions: 1. What did the fairy give the woman?
2. Why was the little girl named Thumbelina?
1. Why did the toad steal Thumbelina?
2. How did Thumbelina escape from the lily pad?
1. How did the cockchafer beetle first treat Thumbelina?
2. Why did the beetle change his mind about Thumbelina?
1. How did Thumbelina survive during summer and autumn?
2. What did Thumbelina have to do to stay with the field mouse?
1. What did Thumbelina discover in the underground tunnels?
2. What did the swallow offer to do when he recovered?
1. Where did the swallow take Thumbelina?
2. Who did Thumbelina discover living in the garden?
assessment: Use chapter comprehension questions for daily checks.
Use journal/writing prompt as written-response evidence.
